did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an ensemble approach combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and texture feature extraction for automatic melanoma detection in dermoscopy images. Specifically, it employed two CNN models (a proposed low-complexity network and VGG-19) for classification, along with kernel principal component analysis (kPCA) for dimensionality reduction of texture features. The system was evaluated on ISIC 2016, ISIC 2019, and PH2 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel ensemble system combining CNNs and texture features for melanoma detection. The paper details specific methods (pre-processing, CNN classification, feature extraction), datasets used (ISIC 2016, ISIC 2019, PH2), and reports original quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) comparing the proposed method against baselines, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Automatic skin cancer detection in dermoscopy images by combining convolutional neural networks and texture features
journal: International Journal of Imaging Systems and Technology
brief_summary: This study proposes an automatic melanoma detection system in dermoscopy images by combining convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and texture features. The ensemble approach utilizes two CNN models, a proposed low-complexity network and VGG-19, alongside texture feature extraction with kernel principal component analysis (kPCA) for dimensionality reduction. The method was evaluated on three datasets: ISIC 2016, ISIC 2019, and PH2. On the ISIC 2016 dataset, the system achieved an accuracy of 85.2%, average precision of 66%, sensitivity of 52%, and specificity of 93.4%. For the ISIC 2019 dataset, the results were 96.7% accuracy, 95.1% average precision, 96.3% sensitivity, and 97.1% specificity. On the PH2 dataset, the method attained 97.5% accuracy, 100% sensitivity, and 96.88% specificity. The experimental results demonstrate that the ensemble method improves evaluation metrics compared to individual phases and outperforms previous studies in melanoma detection performance.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection. The study focuses on automatic melanoma detection. Datasets used include ISIC 2016 (173 melanomas in training, 75 in testing), ISIC 2019 (balanced melanoma/non-melanoma), and PH2 (40 melanoma cases).

The negative class in the binary classification task. In the ISIC 2019 dataset, this category explicitly includes melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, and actinic keratosis. In the PH2 dataset, it includes 160 non-melanoma lesions.
Mentioned as a type of lesion contained within the 'non-melanoma' category of the ISIC 2019 dataset. Not a separate target class in the binary classification task.
Mentioned as a type of lesion contained within the 'non-melanoma' category of the ISIC 2019 dataset. Not a separate target class in the binary classification task.
Mentioned as a type of lesion contained within the 'non-melanoma' category of the ISIC 2019 dataset. Not a separate target class in the binary classification task.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of melanoma vs. non-melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to detect melanoma in dermoscopy images. The system classifies images into two categories: melanoma and non-melanoma. The final diagnosis is obtained by combining results from CNN models and texture feature extraction using an ensemble voting method. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and average precision.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopy image database released by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) for the ISBI challenge in 2016, available on the ISIC archive website. It was used for melanoma detection.

use.training: Used for training the CNN models and feature extraction classifiers.
use.testing: Used for testing the classification performance.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset provided by the Universidade do Porto, Tecnico Lisboa, and the dermatology service of Pedro Hispano hospital in Portugal. It contains dermoscopy images of melanoma and non-melanoma lesions.

use.training: Used in a 10-fold cross-validation scheme for training and testing.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A new version of the ISIC database released in 2019 to evaluate novel methods in melanoma detection, available on the ISIC archive website. A subset of 1600 samples was randomly chosen for this study.

use.training: Used in a threefold cross-validation scheme for training.
use.validation: Used in a threefold cross-validation scheme.
use.testing: Used in a threefold cross-validation scheme for testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for training and testing on the PH2 dataset where training and test images were not predetermined.
Used for training and testing on the ISIC 2019 dataset.
Combined results from CNN models and feature extraction-based methods to obtain the final diagnosis.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made based on absolute values of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and average precision.

ISIC 2016.notes: The ensemble method using VGG-19 and feature extraction achieved the highest Average Precision (0.660) and Accuracy (0.852). The proposed CNN model alone achieved an Accuracy of 0.842. The feature extraction phase with Haralick features and kPCA achieved an Accuracy of 0.813.

PH2.notes: The ensemble method significantly improved Sensitivity from 96.43% (CNN) to 100% and Accuracy from 93.75% to 97.50%. The paper notes that the lowest range of increase was for Specificity (94.49% to 96.88%).

ISIC 2019.notes: The ensemble method increased Accuracy, Average Precision, and Specificity compared to the CNN model alone. The Specificity did not change remarkably between the CNN model and the ensemble method.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of two CNN models (Proposed Low-Complexity CNN and VGG-19) combined with a texture feature extraction phase (Haralick features + LBP + kPCA) and classical classifiers (DT, KNN, SVM, NB, BOOST, ANN).
architecture_used.pretraining: VGG-19 was pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset. The proposed CNN model was trained from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (RGB images converted to gray-level for feature extraction).

layers.notes: Batch Normalization added after this layer
Batch Normalization added after this layer

Batch Normalization added after this layer

proposed_cnn.special_layers: Batch Normalization after layers 1, 2, 4, 6; Dropout after layers 3, 5, 7, 8

vgg19.modifications: Fully connected layers modified to classify lesions in two categories (binary classification) instead of the original 1000 classes.
vgg19.structure_summary: 16 Convolutional layers with 3x3 filters, 5 Max-Pooling layers (2x2), and 3 Fully Connected layers (4096, 4096, 1000 units in original, modified for binary output).

texture_features: Haralick features (derived from Gray-Level Co-Occurrence Matrix in 4 directions: 0, 45, 90, 135 degrees)

training_details.software: Python with Keras deep learning library and TensorFlow backend
training_details.hardware: Intel Core i7 CPU, 64 GB RAM, NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 Ti GPU
training_details.data_augmentation: Applied to training images to enhance robustness and reduce overfitting (specific techniques not detailed, referenced as standard techniques).

classification_or_output_head.cnn_heads: Fully connected layers with 2 units (binary classification output).
classification_or_output_head.ensemble_method: Voting/Comparison of results from CNN models and feature extraction-based method (Algorithm 1).

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB images to gray-level images for texture feature extraction
Data augmentation applied to training images using standard techniques to enhance robustness and reduce overfitting
Feature normalization prior to Kernel Principal Component Analysis (kPCA)
Kernel Principal Component Analysis (kPCA) for dimensionality reduction of texture features

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to training images to enhance robustness and reduce overfitting. Specific techniques and parameters were not detailed in the paper, but they were referenced as standard techniques mentioned in Reference 14.

conclusions: The proposed ensemble method, which combines convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and texture feature extraction, improves evaluation metrics (accuracy, average precision, sensitivity, and specificity) compared to using either phase separately.
The proposed approach increases the performance of melanoma detection compared to previous studies.
On the ISIC 2016 dataset, the ensemble method achieved an accuracy of 85.2%, average precision of 66%, sensitivity of 52%, and specificity of 93.4%.
On the ISIC 2019 dataset, the method achieved an accuracy of 96.7%, average precision of 95.1%, sensitivity of 96.3%, and specificity of 97.1%.
On the PH2 dataset, the method attained an accuracy of 97.5%, sensitivity of 100%, and specificity of 96.88%.
important_remarks: The ISIC 2016 dataset is explicitly noted as unbalanced in melanoma distribution (173 melanomas in training vs. 900 total training images; 75 in testing vs. 350 total), which may complicate diagnosis and bias performance metrics.

The ISIC 2019 dataset used in this study is a randomly selected subset of 1,600 samples from the full database, rather than the entire challenge dataset, which limits the scope of external validation.

The PH2 dataset is small (200 images total) and was evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation, which may lead to optimistic performance estimates due to data leakage between folds if not strictly managed, though the paper reports high sensitivity (100%).

The ensemble voting strategy prioritizes CNN predictions for melanoma cases but relies on feature extraction predictions for non-melanoma cases, creating an asymmetric decision boundary that may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence.
Data augmentation techniques were applied but not explicitly detailed, referencing only 'standard techniques' from another study, which limits reproducibility.

The feature extraction phase alone performed poorly on ISIC 2016 (Sensitivity 0.093), indicating that the ensemble's success is heavily dependent on the CNN component, particularly for detecting the positive class.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to validate the significance of performance improvements over baseline models.

The study focuses solely on binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) and does not address multi-class differentiation among non-melanoma subtypes (e.g., distinguishing basal cell carcinoma from melanocytic nevus), limiting clinical granularity.
